body_txt: Cats having nine lives is an old wives' tale referring to cats' uncanny survival skills. The number of lives for a cat depends though. While in most countries the cat is said to have nine lives, in Arab and Turkish proverbs poor puss has a mere seven lucky lives (something that was then inherited by countries that were invaded by or had contact with Muslims at some point, like Spain and Italy) and in Russia, they are said to survive nine deaths (ergo, ten lives). Why cats are often depicted having nine lives can be explained by the fact that during the ancient times, the number nine was considered a mystical and lucky number, the sum total of the " trinity of trinities ", with the primary alternative, seven, being often considered a lucky or unlucky number, in addition to their associations with magic and their tendency to survive considerable falls—due to what's called a "righting reflex"—and their noted agility seen when they dodge death. In popular culture, though, the expression is used more literally, with cats literally being able to return from death a set number of times. The most common version of this trope depicts cats as having, essentially, eight "get-out-of-death-free" cards: as long as there's at least one spare "life" left, the cat will spring back up good as new no matter how thoroughly it gets flattened, mangled, incinerated, minced, or blown away — a lot like video game lives, in a way. In some cases, a particularly nasty death or shock may eat up all or several of the lives at once. In cartoons, this is traditionally visualised by showing little winged cat souls (with their corresponding number written on them) flying away from the cat when it dies . Cats will also often be seen dying many spectacular deaths until only one life remains . Sometimes the expression is instead taken to mean that cats reincarnate nine times before their souls finally move on to the afterlife, which each life potentially being separated by long periods of time. In these cases, the cat may reincarnate as very different beings or species from notable points of feline history or diversity, such as a saber-toothed cat in the Stone Age, a pampered pharaoh's pet in ancient Egypt, and the like. In both of these cases, cats on their initial few lives will tend to be very flippant about danger and risk. However, when their spare lives start to finally run low — usually when they're on their very last, sometimes when they make the jump from Life 4 to Life 5 or 6 and thus have more spent than spare lives — they may go through a sort of midlife (midlives?) crisis, as it starts to become apparent that they are not, in fact, completely immune to the eventual grip of death. Related to Cats Are Magic . See also Resurrective Immortality and Born-Again Immortality , depending on how the resurrection process works exactly. This trope originally developed strictly around domestic cats, but in modern media is also often extended to other sorts of felids — a leopard, Smilodon , werecat, or Cat Folk are often just as likely to have extra lives in their metaphorical pocket as a witch's familiar.
